sentences.text: So where was I?
And, like I said, I worked in a Thrifty's Drug Store, on Central Avenue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nice $0.75 an hour.
I remember one day we had to work over-time, and it was New Years and it was Sunday.
And I was making $3.75 an hour.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we heard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over here what's going to happen in Israel and Palestine, the fighting.
That there may be a war.
So I wanted to join.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I went to a place here in LA.
It was the office of a place called Habonim.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I saw a lady and I told her I want to join.
I want to go to Israel.
At that time, it was Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she interviewed me, and we talked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got married.
Gloria.
Kuchinsky.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our son was born here, but we still had to go to Israel.
So we want to Israel.
Packed up everything.
We had nothing, but we packed everything up.
Went to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, things, when you talk, you remember.
On the way there, to Israel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we found a man on a boat that didn't have a ticket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was going to be sent back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he wanted to go to Israel.
And they were going to arrest him on the boat and send him back to the United States.
Now we went there with about $50, $60 in our pockets, because we were going to a kibbutz, to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we decided to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] buy him a ticket.
Everybody had to pitch in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To make a long story short, of the $60, I had $10 left when we landed in Haifa.
We landed in Haifa.
People from the kibbutz take-- took us to a place called [INAUDIBLE], which is on the Lebanese border.
My daughter, Leah, was born there.
And we were there for five years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife, my kids, and I--
Jay.
Decided to come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we came back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a Fuller brush man.
I became a salesman.
I became a cook, a waiter, you name it.
One good thing is that I was never out of a job.
I always had a job.
Not one day was I out of a job.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my daughter and my son were asking questions, and I just could not open up.
I couldn't.
The minute they asked the question, I would turn around and walk away.
I would say, leave me alone.
Don't bother me.
They tell me that now.
But they wanted to know, and I couldn't open up.
I couldn't say a word.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, in--
Yeah.
She was born in Chicago.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my daughter said, you must go back to Poland.
She says-- in order to get me there, she says, for my own therapy, for her therapy, I have to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So how many kids have I got?
So I told her, I said, you pay, I'll go.
She paid.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my son is a softy.
He couldn't go.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know.
I guess he's too soft.
Any time I say something now, he starts to cry, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we decided to go.
And she booked the passage, and we went.
You and her?
And my wife.
And by coincidence, it happened that we came to Warsaw on the day that I came back to Warsaw ghetto and couldn't get in, and the day of the uprising, by coincidence.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we got there, we checked into this hotel.
Naturally, I spoke Polish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just so-- for them to know that I understand.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And start asking questions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know what?
Nobody knows anything.
If you can believe that, I'll sell you the Brooklyn Bridge, that they don't know anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody.
I figured I'll speak to older people who were alive at that time.
Nobody.
We don't know.
I said, OK.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We hired a taxi driver.
For $10, he drove us around the whole day.
So we-- I asked him questions.
He was in his 40s, And I talked to him.
And he says, if you think anything has changed, you're wrong.
Nothing has changed.
We still [INAUDIBLE] they will kill somebody.
Anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, how can you say that?
He said, because that's the way they were brought up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We walked around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It says there.
We know now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had a ceremony there.
People from all over the world were there, including Vice President Gore.
I went to the cemetery.
They had a thing at the cemetery there, a service.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, it's a free country now.
It's not the way it was under Russia.
And there [INAUDIBLE] a man with a sign, so wide.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Polish, it says, don't believe it.
This is not true.
Jews did not die in the Warsaw ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I wanted to kill him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he stays there, and people argue with him.
A sign.
Just holding a sign.
And the televisions were there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish televisions, I guess, all over-- all over Europe.
And there were a lot of people starting to argue with him.
I stepped in and I told them, that's exactly what he wants.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wanted somebody to hit him or something so that the television can pick it up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we prevailed.
Nobody said anything.
We just walked away.
We walked away and he had to fold up his paper and walk away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So if anybody tells you that it doesn't exist today, it's worse than it was then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, there were thousands of Jewish children from Israel who came to the Warsaw ghetto memorial.
I'll skip the Warsaw ghetto.
I-- we went to a place called Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And as you arrive in Treblinka, there's a loud speaker that speaks in English and in Polish-- a tape.
And it tells you what went on in Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I went to speak to the custodian there and I told him where I come from.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he took me over to the map and says, there you are.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the back of this building is a huge place with stones, like stone markers, from every village that was perished, every place in the world.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened in Treblinka is they didn't have a crematoria there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had a barbecue.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When you're going to Treblinka, you'll see where the tracks stop-- the railroad tracks.
And all you'll see is, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, grave markers, thousands of them.
In the middle-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was not there, because if I was there, I wouldn't be here-- is a, like, a barbecue, like a block long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, they tell me-- and they have pictures of it-- that there were shelves in the barbecue.
Little shelves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would put the people in there, in the barbecue, after they shot them, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and light the fire.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] When the Russians were coming in, Treblinka is about 80 kilometers east of Warsaw, on the way towards Bialystok.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when the Ger-- when they were coming in, the Germans did away with everything.
There's not-- there was not one building left.
Nothing.
It was a park until the Jewish community of the world found out about it and redid it.
It was nothing.
It was in the middle of the woods, and all you had was empty tracks and that's it.
So I started to speak to people in the village.
I spoke to a person who was maybe 80-- 80 something years old.
And I asked him, were you here?
He says, yeah.
I said, what happened?
He says, I don't know.
I said, what do you mean, you don't know?
You lived here.
Your village is about half a kilometer away.
Half a kilometer is like four blocks.
How can you say you did not see anything?
He says, I didn't see anything.
I said, could you smell anything?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, no.
And he's not the only one.
Most of the people over there were taught from day one to hate everybody but themselves, but mostly Jews.
Why?
I don't know.
I guess it's ignorance.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
From there we went to Auschwitz.
I was not there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But as you walk into Auschwitz, the left hand side is a crematoria, one of them, a small one.
This is not Birkenau.
This is Auschwitz.
And right next to it, about 500 feet away, is a big, beautiful house [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where the camp commandant lived there with his kids-- his wife and kids.
And the crematoria was a half a block away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Poles did do one thing-- or the Russians, for that matter.
They hung the man on the spot.
That they did do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, we went to Birkenau.
And as much as I saw at the first time, I couldn't believe what I saw the second time in Auschwitz, what I saw where I was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What they did in Auschwitz is absolutely unbelievable that human beings can do that.
As you walk into Birkenau, on the right hand side, there are-- there were thousands of barracks.
No barracks left.
The foundation is left.
That's all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the left hand side is where the women lived, where the ladies lived.
That's what they tell me.
I wasn't there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the barracks were the same way as I lived in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The crematorias are gone.
The gas chambers are gone.
But the stench is not gone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's going to be there forever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I hope the Polish people realize it, that it's never going to go away from them.
Why am I so bitter about the Polish?
I'll tell you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because they were conspirators.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They lived on it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] See, at that time, the Catholic church was-- still is, for that matter-- heaven.
But at that time, the Jews killed everybody.
Jews were this.
Jews were that.
Jews killed Jesus.
The Jews--
And being uneducated as they were, they believed it.
You have to be educated to know that there's another li-- story to what you are saying.
And that's why I'm so bitter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, it goes back to my sister.
Germans didn't kill her.
They did.
The Poles did.
Yeah.
I wasn't taken by the Germans.
I was traded in by a Pollack.
All for a bottle of vodka.
So, now you know I am-- why I am the way I am.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I'll tell you-- Birkenau, even though I wasn't there as an inmate, will never leave my nostrils.
Never.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there--
I can smell it.
And I never will forget the smell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there we went to Buchenwald.
My daughter had to see Buchenwald.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm sorry to say, Buchenwald is not Buchenwald.
There's nothing left.
They tell me that the museum that they have there now is-- was a laundry.
I don't remember.
All the barracks are gone.
Where every barrack there is, there's a stump of wood given the number.
I didn't have to have the number.
I knew where I was.
Eight.
Number eight, of the barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you come into camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I swear to God, there's only one thing missing over there.
That's a barbecue, and a table and chair.
That's how nice it is now.
Nothing is left.
And whatever is left is overgrown and fenced off.
You can't get in, like, the crematoria.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did see one thing that I liked, but I didn't believe it.
I saw some college students with their teachers come.
And the teacher would explain what happened.
And you know what they said, the students?
Ich glaube nicht.
I don't believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I-- I'm a nosy man, so I stuck my head in there and I said, believe it.
And they would say, what do you know?
I said, I was here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
With one group, we sat down and we talked there for a few minutes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do speak German, so they understood what I meant.
But there, and this now, in 1993, for them to say, I don't believe it, is something unbelievable.
We didn't stay long there.
From there we went to-- like I told you where I was in [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] on the tower.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I checked into the hotel, the first thing I asked the man, I said, you lived here all your life?
He says, yeah.
Younger man, maybe in his 50s.
So I said, you know anything about a camp here?
He says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're the second person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to ask me.
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I asked him, I said, do you know anything about a camp here, about an Arbeitslager?
He says, you're the third person to ask me about it.
I don't believe it.
I said, believe it.
I was here.
He says, where were you?
I says, come, I'll show you.
He didn't want to go.
I asked him where the street was.
He showed me.
We went out there to that street where it was.
It's now a parking lot where the camp was.
I started to ask people questions.
Nobody knows anything.
I saw one man who was a little older than I was, and I said to him, do you know anything about a bunker here?
I figured, forget about the camp.
Let's talk about a bunker.
He says, when?
I said, in 1944, '45.
You know about a bunker here?
He said, yeah, there was a bunker here down the hill.
He says, I remember.
I played in it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told him, I built it.
I dug it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He said, I don't believe it.
I said, you lived in the bunker.
I dug it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Walked away over there.
One day, asked the people again.
Everyone I asked, the thing was, I don't believe it.
And the only thing I can tell you is if this tape tells anybody-- any man, woman, or child-- what I went through, that's OK.
You see, in 10, 15 years, I'll be gone.
I figure I'm about the youngest alive.
So all the people ahead of me are going to be gone.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So if you don't have this tape to tell people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what the world went through when everybody kept quiet, including the United States, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] please let this be a remembrance.
Thank you.
